rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical selenylation/cyclization of N-aryl acrylamides (and related alkenes) with diphenyl diselenide to access SePh-functionalized oxindoles and isobenzofuran-imines
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh (two sequential SET events) generates phenylselenium cation (PhSe+) and phenylselenium radical (PhSe•); cathode reduces protons to H2

key_intermediates.name: seleniranium ion C (three-membered Se+ adduct on alkene)
benzylic/tertiary carbocation D after ring-opening with SePh on CH2
Wheland-type oxocarbenium/iminium intermediate E from intramolecular nucleophilic attack by carbonyl/amide oxygen

electron_flow: PhSeSePh is oxidized at the anode in two SET steps to PhSe• and PhSe+; PhSe+ adds across the alkene of the N-aryl acrylamide to form a seleniranium ion, which is opened by the pendant carbonyl/amide oxygen (intramolecular nucleophilic cyclization) to give a cationic intermediate; deprotonation furnishes the product while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Selenylation product 3a still obtained in excellent yield, suggesting the major pathway is not radical

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Did not suppress formation of 3a, consistent with a non-radical (ionic) pathway

probe_reagents.name: PhSeCl
probe_reagents.observation: Provided 3a in 43% yield under non-electrochemical conditions, supporting involvement of an electrophilic PhSe+ species (but does not fully exclude a radical pathway)

probe_reagents.name: PhSSPh
probe_reagents.observation: Analogous sulfurized product 9 formed in 45% yield under standard electrochemical conditions, indicating generality of the electrophilic chalcogen pathway

notes: CV showed PhSeSePh oxidizes at 1.416 V and 1.757 V (vs Ag/AgCl), both below substrate 1a (2.101 V), supporting preferential anodic oxidation of the diselenide rather than the alkene. The authors favor an ionic (seleniranium) pathway initiated by anodically generated PhSe+, but explicitly note that a radical pathway involving PhSe• addition cannot be ruled out for other substrate classes (e.g., acrylamides 4 leading to oxindoles 5).
